seoTitle: How to Record Accounts Receivable in QuickBooks Online
metaDescription: Track money owed to your business by creating customer invoices in QuickBooks Online and reviewing your A/R aging report.
intro: Keeping a clear picture of what customers owe you starts with recording every invoice properly. This guide walks through creating a customer invoice in QuickBooks Online, setting payment terms, and running an Accounts Receivable Aging report to see what's outstanding. It's built for business owners who want to stay on top of cash flow.

steps.heading: Open the Invoice form
steps.body: Click + New in the upper left, then choose Invoice under the Customers column.

steps.heading: Select the customer and set terms
steps.body: Choose the client from the customer dropdown, then set payment terms like Net 30 to lock in an official due date.

steps.heading: Add products and services
steps.body: In the line items table, select what you're billing for and enter the correct quantities, rates, and tax classifications.

steps.heading: Attach context and save
steps.body: Add an internal note or attach a related document, then click Save and Send to email the invoice or Save and Close to log it.

steps.heading: Review the A/R Aging report
steps.body: Go to Reports and open the Accounts Receivable Aging Summary to see how your open invoices are distributed by due date.

faq.question: How do payment terms affect my A/R aging?
faq.answer: Terms like Net 30 set the due date QuickBooks uses to bucket the invoice in your aging report, so accurate terms are important for tracking overdue balances.

faq.question: Can I send a reminder for an overdue invoice?
faq.answer: Yes, you can enable automatic reminders or manually resend the invoice from the customer's profile.

faq.question: What if a customer only pays part of the invoice?
faq.answer: You can record a partial payment against the invoice, and the remaining balance will continue to show as open in your A/R.

faq.question: Does creating an invoice affect my income right away?
faq.answer: Yes, invoicing typically increases your Accounts Receivable and recognizes income on an accrual basis, even before the payment is received.
